text: Laparoscopic low anterior resection is an operation performed to remove erectile cancer while sparing the sphincter muscles and reconnecting the bowel. Potentially, this provides a curative operation without a permanent colostomy.
Under general anesthesia, the surgeon uses a laparoscopic camera and two instruments to carefully dissect the lower colon or sigmoid, freeing it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies and marks the left ureter, a tubular structure visible in the surgical field, to prevent injury during the procedure.

The surgeon carefully dissects the mesorectum away from the pelvic bones, blood vessels, and nerves, ensuring the removal of regional lymph nodes along with the rectal tumor for pathological examination.

The surgeon identifies the right ureter before dissecting the right side of the mesorectum. The end of the right fallopian tube is visible above the tip of the cautery instrument. The right hypogastric nerve trunk is identified and preserved, ensuring safe mobilization of the rectum.
The surgeon uses an electrosurgical device to perform the initial dissection, navigating a tissue plane with minimal blood vessels, reducing the risk of bleeding.

The surgeon approaches the pelvic floor during dissection, navigating the increasingly narrow space around the rectum. Careful attention is paid to avoid injury to nearby blood vessels and surrounding organs, including the vagina in women and the prostate gland in men.
The surgeon uses ultrasonic scissors to carry out dissection, effectively sealing small blood vessels while dividing tissues in the pelvic region.

The surgeon performs a digital rectal exam to locate the rectal tumor, followed by the use of a laparoscopic stapling device to divide the rectum below the tumor, ensuring precise separation of the tissue.

The surgeon uses a laparoscopic stapling device that fires six rows of titanium staples to seal both sides of the bowel. The device then divides the bowel with a steel blade between the middle rows. Two applications of the sixty millimeter device are required to completely divide the rectum.

The surgeon selects a point along the sigmoid colon for dissection and division, ensuring the area is mobile enough to reach the remaining rectum and positioned high enough above the tumor to remove the mesorectum lymph nodes. The chosen site is marked with clips.
The surgeon scores the mesentery to mark the line of transection. Large arteries and veins supplying the bowel segment are individually dissected, clipped, and divided using ultrasonic scissors.
The surgeon uses ultrasonic scissors to divide small blood vessels near the bowel, effectively sealing them as the tissues are separated.

The surgeon extends the left lower quadrant laparoscopic incision to four centimeters and inserts a wound protection sleeve. The rectal portion containing the tumor is extracted from the abdomen, and the bowel is divided. The specimen is sent for pathological examination of the tumor and nearby lymph nodes.
The anvil is secured in place, and the wound is closed. The laparoscopic view resumes as the surgeon verifies that the anvil can easily reach the remaining rectum.

The surgeon inserts the main part of the end-to-end anastomotic stapler into the rectum. The center spike shaft is advanced to the rectal stump near the previous staple line. The stapling device is connected, closed, and fired, creating two concentric circles of titanium staples and cutting a hole inside the ring of staples to join the bowel ends.
The surgeon opens the anastomotic stapling device and extracts it, inspecting the shaft for two complete rings of tissue, one from the sigmoid colon and one from the rectum.

A clamp is placed across the lower colon, and the pelvis is filled with saline. The proctoscope is inserted into the rectum, which is then filled with air. The absence of bubbles in the saline visually confirms an airtight seal at the newly formed bowel connection.
The surgeon suctions fluid from the pelvis using a small drain to prevent fluid buildup. Three small incisions are closed with absorbable sutures, marking the completion of the operation.
